structured_choices.data_source: Structured EHR data from a large hospital network spanning five years (60 months). Data includes: (1) admission and discharge timestamps, (2) structured clinical variables (vital signs, lab values, medications, diagnoses coded in ICD-10), (3) patient demographics (age, sex, insurance type), and (4) binary readmission label (readmission within 30 days post-discharge = 1, no readmission or readmission >30 days = 0). Approximately 100,000 discharge episodes. Data quality: missing values are documented; imputation strategy applied post-split (see preprocessing_fit_point). Outliers in vital signs are flagged but retained unless they represent data entry errors (e.g., heart rate >300 bpm); such records are excluded with justification in the analysis. Clinical notes and discharge summaries are excluded from this baseline experiment to ensure structured features only; this allows clean separation of signal from TCN architecture vs. unstructured NLP.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 days of discharge. The observation window begins at discharge timestamp and extends 30 days forward. Any inpatient admission (emergency or planned) to any facility in the hospital network within this window is classified as readmission (label = 1). Readmissions after 30 days or no readmission within 30 days are labeled 0. Transfers within 24 hours of discharge are excluded (these are administrative transfers, not true readmissions). The label is determined after the 30-day window closes, preventing data leakage.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal (chronological) split with stratification on readmission class. The dataset is ordered by discharge timestamp. The first 36 months (Jan Year 1 – Dec Year 3) comprise the training set; months 37–48 (Jan Year 4 – Dec Year 4) comprise the validation set; and months 49–60 (Jan Year 5 – Dec Year 5) comprise the test set. Within each set, stratified sampling ensures the readmission class distribution is approximately preserved (important given class imbalance). Temporal split is justified because: (1) it mimics real deployment where the model must generalize to future patients, (2) it prevents label leakage from future hospitalizations influencing past training data, and (3) it avoids the optimistic bias of random splits where a patient's multiple admissions could appear across train/val/test. Stratification prevents the validation or test sets from accidentally becoming predominantly one class.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36). The fitted transformers are then applied to the validation set (months 37–48) and test set (months 49–60) without refitting. Specifically: (1) missing value imputation (median for numeric features, mode for categorical) is fit on the training set mean/mode, (2) standardization (z-score normalization) is fit on training set mean and standard deviation, (3) categorical encoding (one-hot or ordinal) is fit on training set unique values. This prevents leakage of validation/test statistics into model training. Any new categorical values appearing in validation or test sets are handled as a rare category or dropped; this is documented.
structured_choices.features: Structured features constructed from EHR data over the window [admission to discharge]. Time-series features: (1) last-known values for vital signs (HR, BP, RR, O2 sat, temperature) and lab values (hemoglobin, creatinine, glucose, albumin) at discharge; (2) min/max/mean of vital signs over the hospitalization stay; (3) count and recency of abnormal lab results; (4) length of stay in hours. Static features: (1) age (continuous), sex (binary), insurance type (categorical); (2) Charlson Comorbidity Index computed from ICD-10 diagnosis codes at discharge; (3) number of prior admissions in the past 12 months; (4) number of active medications at discharge; (5) primary discharge diagnosis category (grouped ICD-10 codes). No features directly encode the 30-day post-discharge period. All features are measured or finalized by the discharge timestamp, ensuring they would be available in real deployment.
structured_choices.model: Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) as the intervention model. Architecture: (1) input layer accepts sequences of clinical measurements (e.g., vital signs and lab values) sampled at fixed intervals (daily or admission/discharge checkpoint) over the stay; (2) two residual convolutional blocks with dilated convolutions (dilation rates 1 and 2) to capture multi-scale temporal dependencies; (3) dropout (0.2) for regularization; (4) output layer: global average pooling followed by a dense layer (32 units, ReLU) and sigmoid output for binary classification. Hyperparameters tuned on validation set: filter size (32, 64, 128), kernel size (2, 3), learning rate (0.001–0.01), and dropout rate. Early stopping on validation AUC-ROC with patience=10 epochs.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression on the same structured feature set. Logistic regression is fit to all features without temporal structure (static + aggregated temporal features like min/max/mean). Baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: (1) feature scaling is applied (standardization fit on training set), (2) class weights are balanced to account for imbalance (pos_weight = count_negative / count_positive), (3) L2 regularization strength (lambda) is tuned via grid search on the validation set (lambda in {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}). This ensures the baseline is not disadvantaged; it uses the same feature engineering, same preprocessing, same hyperparameter tuning methodology, and same validation approach. The logistic regression does not use temporal structure by design — this is the contrast being tested.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC). Justification: (1) AUC-ROC is insensitive to the classification threshold, allowing stakeholders to adjust sensitivity/specificity post-hoc based on operational constraints (e.g., how many resources are available for intervention); (2) it handles class imbalance well — unlike accuracy, it does not reward predicting all negatives; (3) it directly reflects the model's ability to rank patients by risk, which is the core operational goal (identifying high-risk patients for targeted intervention). AUC-ROC ranges from 0.5 (random) to 1.0 (perfect). A clinically meaningful improvement is ≥ 0.03 absolute increase (i.e., TCN AUC must exceed logistic regression AUC by at least 0.03).
secondary_metrics: F1-score (macro-averaged) — captures precision-recall tradeoff; important because false negatives (missed high-risk patients) and false positives (unnecessary interventions) both have costs.
Precision and Recall at standard operating points (e.g., 80% sensitivity threshold) — enables assessment of false positive rate if the system triggers interventions at a fixed recall level.

Calibration curve and Brier score — ensures predicted probabilities are well-calibrated; a poorly calibrated model may appear to discriminate well (high AUC) but assign incorrect risk magnitudes, leading to misallocation of resources.

AUC-ROC stratified by key demographic groups (age quartiles, sex, insurance type) — detects whether performance is uniform across populations; identifies potential fairness issues or subgroup-specific confounds.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 37–48). A grid search or random search over the hyperparameter space is conducted; at each iteration, the model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set using AUC-ROC. The hyperparameter configuration that yields the highest validation AUC-ROC is selected. Both TCN and logistic regression baselines use the same validation data for selection, ensuring fairness. The test set (months 49–60) is held out and never touched during model selection or tuning.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is used exactly once, at the end of the experiment, after all model selection and tuning is complete. No exploratory analysis, hyperparameter tuning, or threshold optimization is performed on the test set. The final model (TCN with selected hyperparameters and the baseline logistic regression with selected regularization) is evaluated on the test set using all primary and secondary metrics. Test set results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (computed via bootstrap or cross-validation within the test set if sample size permits). The test set is the sole source of truth for comparing TCN vs. logistic regression.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confounds: patient demographics (age, sex) and underlying health conditions (comorbidities) influence readmission risk. Controls: (1) Demographics and comorbidity index are included as features in both TCN and logistic regression, allowing the models to learn and account for these factors rather than confounding the comparison; (2) Stratified analysis: AUC-ROC is reported separately for age quartiles, sex, and comorbidity risk strata (low/medium/high Charlson score). This reveals whether the TCN advantage (if any) is uniform across subgroups or concentrated in specific populations, informing generalizability; (3) Temporal confounds (e.g., seasonal effects, changes in hospital protocols over five years): the temporal split naturally mixes seasons and years across all sets, but long-term protocol changes may bias later test sets. Sensitivity analysis: a second evaluation is performed on a held-out cohort from Year 4 only (concurrent validation and test data), checking if results replicate within a shorter timeframe; (4) Missing data mechanism: if missingness is not random (e.g., sicker patients have more frequent lab draws), this is documented; missingness patterns are compared across train/val/test to ensure consistency. Any systematic differences trigger a re-analysis with missingness indicators as features. The experiment does not establish causality—it establishes whether TCN outperforms logistic regression in predictive performance on held-out data, under the assumption that both models receive comparable feature engineering, tuning, and evaluation effort.

design_narrative:
To test whether Temporal Convolutional Networks outperform logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction, the following experimental design is proposed.

**1. Data and Label Definition**
The experiment uses five years of structured EHR data from the hospital network, comprising approximately 100,000 discharge episodes. Raw data includes clinical variables (vital signs, labs, medications, diagnoses), demographics, and admission/discharge timestamps. The outcome is binary: readmission to any facility within the hospital network within 30 days of discharge = 1; no readmission or readmission >30 days = 0. Transfers within 24 hours are excluded as administrative rather than true readmissions. The label is finalized after the 30-day observation window closes, preventing label leakage.

**2. Data Splitting Strategy**
A temporal split is used to reflect real deployment and prevent leakage. Data are ordered by discharge timestamp and divided into: Training (months 1–36), Validation (months 37–48), and Test (months 49–60). Within each set, stratified sampling preserves the readmission class distribution, mitigating class imbalance effects during model selection. Temporal splitting is essential because it prevents future information from biasing model training and ensures the test set represents genuinely held-out future patients—the true generalization target.

**3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing**
Structured features are engineered from the admission-to-discharge window: temporal aggregates (min, max, mean of vital signs; recency of abnormal labs; length of stay), discharge-time snapshots (last-known vitals and labs), and static variables (age, sex, insurance, comorbidity index, prior admission count, active medication count, discharge diagnosis). All preprocessing (imputation, standardization, encoding) is fit solely on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage of validation/test statistics into model training.

**4. Model and Baseline**
The intervention model is a Temporal Convolutional Network with residual blocks (dilated convolutions, dilation rates 1–2), dropout regularization, and sigmoid output. The baseline is logistic regression on the same structured features, with identical preprocessing and equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set. Both models use class-weighted loss to account for imbalance. The baseline does not exploit temporal structure—this design contrast is the core comparison.

**5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection**
For the TCN: filter sizes (32–128), kernel size (2–3), learning rate (0.001–0.01), and dropout (0.2) are tuned via grid search on the validation set, optimizing AUC-ROC. For logistic regression: L2 regularization strength is tuned on the same validation set. The configuration yielding highest validation AUC-ROC is selected for each model. The test set is never used during tuning.

**6. Evaluation Metrics**
Primary metric: AUC-ROC on the test set. This metric is threshold-agnostic, handles class imbalance appropriately, and directly reflects the operational objective of ranking patients by readmission risk. A clinically meaningful improvement is ≥0.03 absolute AUC difference. Secondary metrics include F1-score (macro), precision, recall at standard operating points, calibration curve, and Brier score. Stratified analysis: AUC-ROC is reported separately by age quartile, sex, and comorbidity risk level to detect confounding or subgroup effects.

**7. Controls and Confounds**
Demographics and comorbidity burden are included as model features, allowing both models to account for these confounds rather than have them bias the comparison. Long-term protocol changes are assessed via sensitivity analysis on a Year-4-only cohort. Missing data mechanisms are documented; if missingness is non-random and differs across train/val/test, missingness indicators are added as features.

**8. Test Set and Finalization**
The test set (months 49–60) is used exactly once, after all model selection is complete. Final metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap. The result establishes whether TCN outperforms logistic regression in predictive performance on held-out data under matched experimental conditions; it does not establish causality or deployment safety without additional validation.
